skills: reverse dialogue completion (supply the missing question) - now the dominant dialogue format (majority of Part 1 items)
proposal-plus-justification dialogue logic (agreement leads to a stated benefit/reason)
deferred-question/scheduling exchange (uncertain time, will confirm later)
WRONG-punctuation identification (reverse framing of the usual correct-punctuation task)
multi-constraint budget/menu word problem (allowance + required-purchase rules + day-of-week restriction combined)
weather-forecast practical-implication reasoning (what item is needed on a given day)
chart-literacy meta-question (what information CAN be read from a bar chart)
science-diagram vocabulary (water cycle: evaporation, condensation, precipitation)
environmental science passage (PM2.5) with a CORRECT-statement question
narrative/aspiration passage with a reason-inference "why" question
cross-cultural comparison reading passage (Black Friday US vs UK) with TRUE and NOT-mentioned/false question variants
NOT-mentioned exclusion logic in a short cultural passage
grammar: punctuation: exclamation marks, colon before a list, comma placement
modal/conditional requests (Would you mind turning on the lights)
question_types: reverse dialogue completion (fill in the question) - majority format in Part 1
standard dialogue completion (fill in the reply)
chart-literacy meta-question (what can be read from this chart)
environmental science passage with a CORRECT-statement question
cross-cultural comparison passage with TRUE and NOT-mentioned questions
reading_patterns: cross-cultural comparison passage (~90-110 words, 2 short paragraphs) contrasting the same named event/custom between two countries, followed by both a direct-detail question and a TRUE/NOT-true synthesis question
short aspirational narrative (~60-80 words) about a child's daily routine and future goal, followed by a reason-inference "why" question with passage-adjacent-but-unsupported distractors
environmental/science short passage (~40-60 words) followed by a CORRECT-statement selection question
dialogue_patterns: reverse-completion pattern dominant: worried/troubled statement -> student supplies the clarifying question
proposal-plus-justification: an agreement phrase ("That's true") precedes the speaker elaborating their own suggestion's benefit
deferred-answer/scheduling exchange (uncertain about exact time, promises to confirm)
constraint-satisfaction word-problem embedded in a menu+budget scenario requiring combining an allowance limit, a required-purchase rule, and a day-of-week restriction
distractor_patterns: wrong reverse-question that would elicit a plausible but different reply
vowel-sound distractor sharing spelling pattern but not phoneme (e.g. gives vs drive)
punctuation distractor that looks superficially fine but violates a specific rule (list requires a colon, not a comma)
budget-constraint distractor violating one hidden rule (e.g. choosing dessert on a non-Friday, or choosing soda)
NOT-mentioned distractor that is topically plausible but never stated in the passage
fixed universal claim distractor in TRUE/FALSE cultural-comparison items ("Black Friday means the same thing in both countries")
answer_logic: reverse-completion items require inferring the exact question type (wh- word and topic) that is uniquely consistent with the given answer
multi-constraint word problems require satisfying every stated rule simultaneously (budget ceiling, required categories, day-restricted categories), not just the arithmetic total
cross-cultural TRUE/NOT-true items require checking a claim against both halves of a 2-paragraph comparison, since a statement can be true for one country and false for the other
reason-inference "why" items require identifying the specific stated cause in the passage rather than any passage-adjacent detail
notes: Exam sat 31 Jan 2569 (BE+1 sitting-date convention). Explicit 'Part 1: Language Use (Items 1-17)' / 'Part 2: Reading (Items 18-32)' structure, continuing from 2567. Reverse dialogue-completion is now the majority pattern in Part 1 (a clear escalation from 2567). Most recent and most structurally/cognitively demanding year analyzed; represents the current-state target difficulty and format for new question generation.
